The purpose and plan of John's ministry were to lead people away from their sins and toward their Savior. He came preaching a way of living, not a group or faction; the sign or ceremony was washing with water. Through these words, John preached that repentance was necessary for forgiveness of sins, and that baptism with water was an outward sign of that inner cleansing and renewal of heart, which accompanies or results from true repentance as well as a profession of it. This fulfills Scripture in John’s ministry. When the way is prepared for the gospel into the heart, by rejecting proud thoughts and bringing them into obedience to Christ, by leveling the soul, and removing all that hinders us in the way of Christ and his grace, then preparation is made to welcome God's salvation. Here are general warnings and exhortations that John gave. The guilty, corrupted race of mankind has become a generation of vipers; hateful to God, and hating one another. There is no way to escape God’s wrath except through repentance; and our change in behavior must show a change in our thinking. If we are not truly holy, both in heart and life, our profession of religion and relationship to God and his church will be useless; our destruction will be even greater if we do not produce fruit that shows we have repented. John the Baptist gave instructions to various kinds of people. Those who profess and promise repentance must show it by reforming their behavior, according to their positions and circumstances. The gospel requires mercy, not sacrifice; and its purpose is to encourage us to do all the good we can, and to be just to everyone. And the same principle that leads people to abandon dishonest gain also leads them to restore what was gained through wrongdoing. John told the soldiers their duty. People should be warned against the temptations of their jobs. These answers declared the present duty of those who were seeking guidance, and at once served as a test of their sincerity. Just as no one can or will accept Christ’s salvation without true repentance, so the evidence and effects of this repentance are here shown.

John the Baptist denied that he himself was the Christ, but confirmed people's expectations of the long-promised Messiah. He could only urge them to repent, and assure them of forgiveness upon repentance; but he could not cause them to repent, nor grant them forgiveness. This is how we should speak about Christ, and this is how humbly we should speak about ourselves. John can do no more than baptize with water, as a sign that they ought to purify and cleanse themselves; but Christ can, and will baptize with the Holy Spirit; he can give the Spirit, to cleanse and purify the heart, not only as water washes off dirt on the outside, but as fire clears out impurities within, and melts down metal so it can be cast into a new form. John was an enthusiastic preacher; he pleaded earnestly; he emphasized things strongly with his listeners. He was a practical preacher; quickening them to their duty, and directing them in it. He was a popular preacher; he addressed the people according to their understanding. He was an evangelical preacher. In all his exhortations, he directed people to Christ for both righteousness and strength. He was a prolific preacher; he did not shy away from declaring the whole counsel of God. But John’s preaching stopped when he was in the midst of his usefulness. Herod, being rebuked by him for many evils, imprisoned John. Those who harm God's faithful servants add even greater guilt to their other sins.

Christ did not confess sin, as others did, because he had none to confess; but he prayed, as others did, and maintained fellowship with his Father. Observe that all three voices from heaven, by which the Father bore witness to the Son, were spoken while he was praying, or shortly after. The Holy Spirit descended in a visible form like a dove upon him, and there came a voice from heaven, from God the Father, from the excellent glory. This demonstrated the Holy Trinity, of the Three Persons in the Godhead, at Christ’s baptism.

Matthew's list of Jesus' ancestors showed that Christ was the son of Abraham, through whom all families of the earth are blessed, and heir to the throne of David; but Luke shows that Jesus was the Seed of the woman who would crush the serpent's head, and traces the line back to Adam, beginning with Eli, or Heli, the father, not of Joseph, but of Mary. The apparent differences between the two evangelists in these lists of names have been resolved by scholars. But our salvation does not depend on our ability to solve these difficulties, nor is the divine authority of the Gospels weakened by them. The list of names ends with, “Who was the son of Adam, the son of God;” that is, the offspring of God by creation. Christ was both the son of Adam and the Son of God, so he could be a proper mediator between God and the sons of Adam, and bring the sons of Adam to be, through him, the sons of God. All flesh, as descended from the first Adam, is like grass, and withers like the flower of the field; but he who shares in the Holy Spirit of life from the Second Adam has that eternal happiness which is preached to us by the gospel.
